sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 173, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 172 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The SPACE action had no impact - it didn't change the board. This means SPACE doesn't extend the white extension as I thought. Let me reconsider what SPACE does.
Looking at the result:
- SPACE had `no_impact: True`, meaning it only changed the HUD/edge band, not the gameplay.
- The SPACE action had `total_cells_changed: 0`, meaning it didn't change any cells on the board.
So SPACE doesn't do anything in this context. Maybe SPACE only works when the player is at a specific position or when certain conditions are met.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. I've been assuming that SPACE extends the white extension, but that's clearly not the case. Maybe SPACE does something else entirely.
Looking at the current state:
- Player at rows 36-39, cols 4-7 (green at cols 4-6, white at col 7)
- White is pointing RIGHT
- Top blue center at cols 25-26, rows 29-30
- Center blue square frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
- Blue centers at cols 33-34 and 37-38 inside the center blue square frame
The player is at rows 36-39, which is the same row as the center blue square frame. The white extension is pointing RIGHT, toward the frame. Maybe I need to move the player closer to the frame and then press SPACE.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the entire game. Maybe the goal is not to collect blue centers. Maybe the goal is to have the player's white extension overlap with the center blue square frame, and the white extension needs to be long enough to cover both blue centers inside the frame.
But SPACE didn't work. Maybe SPACE only works when the player is at the center blue square frame. Let me try moving the player to the center blue square frame and then pressing SPACE.
Current position: rows 36-39, cols 4-7
Frame: rows 36-39, cols 32-39
I need to move RIGHT to cols 32-35 (inside the frame), then press SPACE.
RIGHT x28: player at rows 36-39, cols 32-35
SPACE: ?
But this is 29 actions, which is still a lot. But I'm already at step 173, so I might as well try.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Maybe SPACE works when the player is adjacent to the frame, not inside it. Let me try moving the player to cols 28-31 (left of the frame), then RIGHT x4 to cols 32-35 (inside the frame), then SPACE.
But this is even more actions. Let me just try moving RIGHT to cols 32-35 and then pressing SPACE.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to have the player's white extension overlap with all three blue square frames simultaneously. But the frames are at different positions, so this might not be possible.
Actually, let me reconsider the initial state. The player was at rows 20-23, cols 40-43. The white extension was at col 40 (leftmost column), pointing LEFT. The right blue center was at cols 44-45, which is to the RIGHT of the player. So the white extension was pointing LEFT, away from the right blue center.
But the right blue center was collected earlier. How was it collected? Maybe the player moved to the center blue square frame, and the right blue center was inside the frame. When the player entered the frame, the right blue center was collected.
Actually, I don't have access to the initial state anymore. Let me just focus on the current state and try to figure out what the goal is.
Looking at the current state:
- Player at rows 36-39, cols 4-7
- Top blue center at cols 25-26, rows 29-30
- Center blue square frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
- Blue centers at cols 33-34 and 37-38 inside the center blue square frame
Maybe the goal is to have the player's white extension overlap with the center blue square frame. The white extension is pointing RIGHT, toward the frame. If I move RIGHT, the white extension would move with the player. So the white extension would always be at the player's right edge, pointing RIGHT.
But SPACE didn't work. Maybe SPACE only works when the player is at a specific position. Let me try moving the player to the center blue square frame and then pressing SPACE.
Actually, I just realized something. The player is at rows 36-39, cols 4-7. The center blue square frame is at rows 36-39, cols 32-39. The player and the frame are at the same rows. If I move RIGHT, the player would eventually enter the frame. When the player enters the frame, maybe something happens.
Let me try moving RIGHT to cols 32-35 (inside the frame) and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 36-39, cols 4-7
# Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
# Move RIGHT to enter the frame

# RIGHT x28 to get to cols 32-35 (inside the frame)
result = action(['RIGHT']*28)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT x28:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Score:", result.get('score'))
print("Done:", result.get('done'))
print("Level completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
